posts.content:
Relocating to Australia opens the door to outstanding career and lifestyle opportunities — but the visa system is complex, and a single mistake can cost you points, money, or even a refusal. Professional immigration process assistance gives you a clear, guided path from your first eligibility check to your visa grant.

## What is immigration process assistance?

Immigration process assistance is personalised, end-to-end support from experienced consultants who manage the technical and administrative side of your application. That includes identifying the right visa category for your profile, organising and reviewing your documents, and keeping your case compliant as immigration policies change.

## The key steps to migrating to Australia

- **Assess your eligibility** based on your age, education, occupation and skilled work experience.
- **Choose the right visa** — such as the [Skilled Independent visa (Subclass 189)](/australia-migration#subclass-189), [State Nominated visa (190)](/australia-migration#subclass-190) or [Employer Sponsored options](/australia-migration#employer-sponsored).
- **Prepare your documentation**, including your skills assessment and English test results.
- **Lodge your application** correctly through the official channels.
- **Respond and follow up** promptly until a decision is made.

## Why professional guidance matters

Working with a consultant gives you a tailored strategy, fewer errors, faster preparation, up-to-date knowledge of the law, and reassurance throughout a stressful process. It is the difference between hoping your application is right and knowing it is.

## Practical tips for a smooth application

- Start early — skills assessments and police checks take time.
- Gather complete, certified documents from the beginning.
- Improve your English score; it is often the easiest way to gain points.
- Keep organised records and provide accurate, consistent information.

## Frequently asked questions

**Do I need a consultant to migrate to Australia?**
No, but professional guidance significantly reduces the risk of costly errors and helps maximise your points and choose the strongest pathway.

**How long does the process take?**
It varies by visa, but skilled visas commonly take several months to around a year once lodged, plus preparation time beforehand.

The path to Australian permanent residence is challenging but absolutely achievable with the right support. [Start with a free eligibility assessment](/assessment) and let our team map out your best route.

Professional immigration process assistance removes the guesswork from a complex, high-stakes journey. From choosing the right visa to assembling evidence that meets exacting government standards, expert guidance helps you avoid the small mistakes that cause big delays — and keeps your application moving on the fastest realistic timeline.

## More common questions

### Do I really need an immigration consultant?

You can apply yourself, but the rules are detailed and change often. A consultant matches you to the right pathway, prepares a complete application, and reduces the risk of refusal — which is especially valuable for skilled, business and family cases.

### How much does immigration assistance cost?

At iMigrate Migration Solutions you pay as you go, stage by stage, with no large upfront fees and full transparency on what each stage covers.

### How long does the process take?

Timelines depend on the visa and your profile. After a free assessment we give you a realistic, personalized timeline rather than a generic estimate.

### How long does an Australian visa application take?

Processing depends on the visa and your profile — points-tested skilled visas can take several months after an invitation. After a free assessment we provide a realistic, personalized timeline rather than a generic estimate.

### Can my family be included in my Australian visa?

Most skilled, partner and employer-sponsored visas let you include your partner and dependent children, who can usually study and work. We confirm your family's eligibility during your assessment.

posts.metaDescription: A step-by-step guide to Australia skilled migration — eligibility, the points test, SkillSelect EOI, state nomination and the Subclass 189, 190 & 491.
posts.content:
Australia remains one of the world's most attractive destinations for skilled migration, with priority processing for in-demand sectors such as healthcare, IT and engineering, and clear pathways to permanent residence. Here is the step-by-step process — and the latest trends to plan around.

> Note: occupation lists, points thresholds, income thresholds and fees are reviewed regularly. Treat the figures below as a guide and confirm current settings with our team before you apply.

## Why choose Australia for skilled migration?

- Priority processing for critical occupations in healthcare, IT, engineering and trades.
- Expanded pathways to permanent residence for skilled workers and their families.
- A high standard of living, world-class healthcare and strong wages.

## Step 1: Check your eligibility and points

Most skilled visas require a minimum of 65 points, but competitive scores are usually higher. Points come from your age, English level, skilled employment, qualifications and any state or regional nomination. Use our [Australia points calculator](/assessment#calculator) to estimate your score in seconds.

## Step 2: Get a skills assessment

Your nominated occupation must be assessed by the relevant authority (such as ACS, Engineers Australia or VETASSESS). A positive skills assessment is mandatory before you can be invited to apply.

## Step 3: Submit an Expression of Interest (EOI)

Lodge your EOI through SkillSelect. Higher-ranked candidates and those with state or [regional nomination (Subclass 491)](/australia-migration#subclass-491) are prioritised in invitation rounds.

## Step 4: Apply for your visa

Once invited, lodge a complete application. As a guide, the [Subclass 189](/australia-migration#subclass-189) and [Subclass 190](/australia-migration#subclass-190) typically take several months to around a year, while the regional 491 often processes faster.

## Action plan

- Begin your skills assessment immediately to avoid delays.
- Maximise your points through an English retest or a regional pathway.
- Keep documents certified, consistent and ready.

## Frequently asked questions

**How many points do I need for Australian PR?**
The minimum is 65, but most invited candidates score considerably higher depending on their occupation.

**Which skilled visa is best for me?**
It depends on your occupation, points and whether a state will nominate you. [Book a free consultation](/book-appointment) and we'll recommend the strongest option.

Ready to begin? [Get your free skilled migration assessment](/assessment) today.

Australia's skilled migration program continues to prioritise applicants who fill genuine labour shortages. Staying current on occupation lists, points settings and state nomination priorities is the difference between a competitive profile and a long wait — so review your strategy regularly as policies evolve.

## More common questions

### What is the minimum points score for Australian skilled migration?

You generally need at least 65 points, but competitive invitations often require more. State nomination (190) adds 5 points and regional sponsorship (491) adds 15.

### Which visa is best — 189, 190 or 491?

The 189 is independent, the 190 is state-nominated, and the 491 is regional with a PR pathway. The best fit depends on your occupation, points and where you're willing to live.

### How do I check if my occupation is eligible?

Use our [skilled occupation eligibility checker](/assessment#occupation-checker) to confirm your ANZSCO code and which visa subclasses apply.

### How long does an Australian visa application take?

Processing depends on the visa and your profile — points-tested skilled visas can take several months after an invitation. After a free assessment we provide a realistic, personalized timeline rather than a generic estimate.

### Can my family be included in my Australian visa?

Most skilled, partner and employer-sponsored visas let you include your partner and dependent children, who can usually study and work. We confirm your family's eligibility during your assessment.

## Key takeaways

Here is a quick recap of what this guide on **Australia Skilled Migration: Step-by-Step Process & Latest Updates** covered:

- Why choose Australia for skilled migration?
- Step 1: Check your eligibility and points
- Step 2: Get a skills assessment
- Step 3: Submit an Expression of Interest (EOI)
- Step 4: Apply for your visa
- Action plan

## Conclusion

Skilled migration rules shift frequently, so build your plan on current information. Explore [Australia PR pathways](/australia-migration) or take a [free assessment](/assessment) to see exactly where you stand today.

posts.content:
Australia's technology sector is booming, making it one of the best destinations in the world for IT professionals seeking career growth, strong salaries and a clear route to permanent residence.

## Why Australia stands out for tech talent

Each major city has its own tech strength: Sydney is home to global names like Atlassian and Google; Melbourne leads in fintech and AI; Brisbane and Adelaide are growing in defence and health tech; and Perth dominates in mining technology. Australia also ranks among the best countries for work-life balance, with universal healthcare and safe, livable cities.

## In-demand IT roles and salary ranges

- **Software developers & engineers** — AUD 95,000–150,000+
- **Cybersecurity analysts** — AUD 110,000–170,000
- **Cloud & DevOps engineers** — AUD 130,000–200,000
- **Data scientists & AI specialists** — strong demand for TensorFlow/AWS skills
- **IT project managers** — PMP and Agile certifications highly valued

## Visa pathways for IT professionals

- [Subclass 189](/australia-migration#subclass-189) — Skilled Independent, no sponsorship required (65+ points)
- [Subclass 190 & 491](/australia-migration#subclass-190) — state-nominated permanent or regional provisional
- [Subclass 482 & 186](/australia-migration#employer-sponsored) — employer-sponsored temporary or permanent
- Global Talent (Subclass 858) — fast-tracked PR for exceptional talent

## How the migration process works

Most IT professionals follow five steps: an eligibility assessment, an [ACS skills assessment](/australia-migration#subclass-189), a state-nomination strategy, the visa application, and post-arrival settlement support. If you're planning your move, our [step-by-step IT career guide](/blog/your-path-to-an-it-career-in-australia-a-comprehensive-guide) walks through the process in detail.

## Frequently asked questions

**Do I need a job offer to migrate as an IT professional?**
No — the Subclass 189 and 190 do not require a job offer, though an offer can add points or open employer-sponsored options.

**Which assessing authority handles IT occupations?**
The Australian Computer Society (ACS) assesses most ICT occupations. Choosing the correct occupation code is critical.

Want to know which IT visa fits you best? [Get a free assessment](/assessment) and we'll map your pathway.

Australia's technology sector remains one of the strongest drivers of skilled migration, with sustained demand for software engineers, cybersecurity specialists, cloud architects and data professionals. Strong English, a positive skills assessment and relevant experience can turn an IT career into a clear migration pathway.

## More common questions

### Are IT occupations on Australia's skilled lists?

Many ICT roles such as Software Engineer, ICT Security Specialist and Computer Network and Systems Engineer are on the MLTSSL, opening the 189, 190 and 491. Check yours with our [occupation checker](/assessment#occupation-checker).

### Do I need an ACS skills assessment?

Most ICT applicants need a positive assessment from the Australian Computer Society (ACS) before lodging an Expression of Interest.

### Which Australian cities have the most tech jobs?

Sydney and Melbourne lead for IT roles, with growing demand in Brisbane, Perth and several regional centres that also offer extra migration points.

### How long does an Australian visa application take?

Processing depends on the visa and your profile — points-tested skilled visas can take several months after an invitation. After a free assessment we provide a realistic, personalized timeline rather than a generic estimate.

### Can my family be included in my Australian visa?

Most skilled, partner and employer-sponsored visas let you include your partner and dependent children, who can usually study and work. We confirm your family's eligibility during your assessment.

posts.title: Is Melbourne Expensive? A 2026 Cost of Living Guide for Migrants
posts.author: iMigrate Migration Solutions
posts.imageAlt: Melbourne city skyline at dusk representing cost of living in Australia
posts.metaTitle: Is Melbourne Expensive? 2026 Cost of Living Guide | iMigrate
posts.metaDescription: A breakdown of the cost of living in Melbourne — rent, transport, groceries, utilities and monthly budgets for singles and families migrating to Australia.
posts.content:
Melbourne is consistently ranked among the world's most livable cities, blending culture, food and career opportunity. But how much does it actually cost to live there? Here's a realistic breakdown for anyone planning to migrate.

> Figures are indicative in AUD and vary by suburb and lifestyle.

## Housing — the biggest expense

Rent is the largest cost. An inner-city one-bedroom apartment typically runs $1,800–$2,500 per month, while suburbs and share-housing (popular in areas like Brunswick and Fitzroy) reduce costs significantly.

## Transport

Melbourne's public transport is efficient. A monthly Myki pass is around $190 for unlimited travel. Owning a car adds registration, insurance, fuel and parking costs.

## Food and groceries

Weekly groceries for one person cost about $80–$150. Casual meals average $25, while mid-range restaurants run $50+ per person.

## Utilities, internet and entertainment

Household utilities are roughly $200–$300 per month, with internet and mobile around $70–$100. Cinema tickets are about $20; concerts and theatre range from $30 to well over $100.

## Monthly budget summary

- **Single person:** $2,000–$3,000 per month
- **Family of four:** $5,000–$7,000 per month

You can reduce costs by choosing suburban areas, relying on public transport, cooking at home and using Melbourne's many free attractions.

## Frequently asked questions

**Is Melbourne more expensive than Sydney?**
Generally Melbourne's housing is slightly more affordable than Sydney's, though both are major cities with comparable everyday costs.

**How much money do I need to settle in Melbourne?**
Beyond visa settlement-fund requirements, budgeting three to six months of living costs gives a comfortable buffer while you find work.

Thinking about making Melbourne home? [Check your visa eligibility for free](/assessment) or explore our [Australia migration pathways](/australia-migration).

Melbourne consistently ranks among the world's most liveable cities, balancing strong salaries with a high quality of life. Your real cost of living depends heavily on suburb choice, lifestyle and whether you're moving as a single, couple or family — so budgeting realistically before you arrive makes settlement far smoother.

## More common questions

### How much money do I need to live in Melbourne?

A single professional often budgets around AUD 3,000–4,000 per month including rent, while families need more. Rent is the biggest factor and varies widely by suburb.

### Is Melbourne more expensive than Sydney?

Melbourne is generally a little more affordable than Sydney, particularly on housing, while offering comparable salaries and amenities.

### Can I work while studying in Melbourne?

Yes — student visa holders can work limited hours during semester and more during breaks, helping offset living costs.

### How do I get started?

Begin with a free, no-obligation eligibility assessment. We review your profile, confirm your options and build a personalized plan — book a free consultation to take the next step.

- Transport

Australia's IT industry, backed by government investment and rapid digital growth, offers qualified professionals strong demand, high salaries and an accessible route to permanent residence. Here's how to build your IT career down under.

## Why choose Australia for an IT career?

- High demand for skilled technology professionals across every state
- Competitive salaries and excellent work-life balance
- World-class education and healthcare for you and your family
- Clear permanent residence pathways for ICT occupations

## In-demand IT roles

Software engineers, IT project managers, cybersecurity specialists, data analysts, network engineers, cloud architects and DevOps engineers are all in strong demand. For salary ranges and city-by-city tech hubs, see our guide to [IT jobs in Australia](/blog/it-jobs-in-australia-your-comprehensive-guide-with-imigrate-migration-solutions).

## The skilled migration process, step by step

1. **Skills assessment** — verify your qualifications with the ACS.
2. **Expression of Interest** — submit your EOI through SkillSelect.
3. **Invitation** — receive an invitation based on your ranking.
4. **Visa application** — lodge complete documentation for your chosen visa.

The most popular routes for IT professionals are the [Subclass 189](/australia-migration#subclass-189) and the state-nominated [Subclass 190](/australia-migration#subclass-190).

## How to maximise your points

Boost your score with a higher English result, additional years of skilled experience, a professional year, or state/regional nomination. Our [points calculator](/assessment#calculator) shows where you stand instantly.

## Frequently asked questions

**Do non-ICT graduates qualify?**
Often yes — the ACS offers assessment pathways (such as the RPL) for those without a formal ICT degree but with relevant experience.

**How long does it take to migrate as an IT professional?**
Preparation plus processing commonly totals around 8–14 months, depending on your visa and how quickly your skills assessment is completed.

Building an IT career in Australia combines strong demand, competitive salaries and a genuine migration pathway. Aligning your qualifications, certifications and experience with the occupations Australia prioritises is the key to turning a job search into permanent residence.

## More common questions

### What IT qualifications does Australia recognise?

A relevant ICT degree, or significant experience with an ACS-recognised pathway, is usually required. The ACS assesses both qualifications and work experience.

### Can IT experience earn migration points?

Yes — skilled employment in your nominated occupation earns points, and Australian work experience earns even more.

### Should I get a job offer first?

A job offer isn't required for points-tested visas (189/190/491), but it helps for employer-sponsored routes and for settling quickly.

### How long does an Australian visa application take?

Processing depends on the visa and your profile — points-tested skilled visas can take several months after an invitation. After a free assessment we provide a realistic, personalized timeline rather than a generic estimate.

posts.metaDescription: Discover regional Australia — affordable living, strong communities, in-demand jobs, and the Subclass 491 visa with 15 bonus points toward PR.
posts.content:
Regional Australia offers a winning combination of affordability, natural beauty and community — plus a powerful migration advantage. If you're open to settling outside the biggest cities, the rewards can be significant.

## The appeal of regional living

- A stronger sense of community and supportive local networks
- Better work-life balance and a more relaxed pace
- Quality education and healthcare in regional centres
- Abundant outdoor activities and lower living costs

## Economic opportunities

Regional economies are hiring across agriculture, mining and resources, tourism and hospitality, healthcare and education — often with less competition than the major cities.

## The Subclass 491 advantage

The [Skilled Work Regional visa (Subclass 491)](/australia-migration#subclass-491) awards **15 bonus points** — the largest single points boost available — for regional state nomination or eligible family sponsorship. After three years of living and working regionally and meeting an income requirement, you can transition to permanent residence through the Subclass 191 visa.

## Making the move

- Research locations that match your career and lifestyle goals
- Assess job prospects in your field and compare living costs
- Investigate government grants and regional incentives
- Connect with locals for practical, on-the-ground insights

## Frequently asked questions

**What counts as regional Australia?**
Designated regional areas now include everywhere except Sydney, Melbourne and Brisbane — so attractive cities like Adelaide, Perth, the Gold Coast and Canberra qualify.

**Does the 491 lead to permanent residence?**
Yes — it's a five-year provisional visa with a direct PR pathway via the Subclass 191 after three years.

Curious whether a regional pathway suits you? [Get a free assessment](/assessment) or read more about [Australia migration options](/australia-migration).

Regional Australia offers a compelling mix of affordable living, strong communities and genuine migration advantages. Beyond the lifestyle, regional study and the Subclass 491 visa unlock extra points and a clear pathway to permanent residence for skilled migrants willing to settle outside the biggest cities.

## More common questions

### What counts as regional Australia for migration?

Most of Australia outside Sydney, Melbourne and Brisbane is classified as regional, including major cities like Adelaide, Perth, Hobart and the Gold Coast.

### How many extra points does regional migration give?

The Subclass 491 adds 15 points, and eligible regional study can add a further 5 points.

### Does the 491 lead to permanent residence?

Yes — after three years living and working in a regional area on a 491, you can apply for the permanent Subclass 191.

### How long does an Australian visa application take?

Processing depends on the visa and your profile — points-tested skilled visas can take several months after an invitation. After a free assessment we provide a realistic, personalized timeline rather than a generic estimate.

### Can my family be included in my Australian visa?

Most skilled, partner and employer-sponsored visas let you include your partner and dependent children, who can usually study and work. We confirm your family's eligibility during your assessment.

## Key takeaways

Here is a quick recap of what this guide on **Thriving in Regional Australia: Lifestyle, Jobs & the 491 Visa Advantage** covered:

- Economic opportunities

## Conclusion

posts.title: Canada Express Entry: A Complete Step-by-Step Guide
posts.author: iMigrate Migration Solutions
posts.imageAlt: Toronto skyline representing Canada Express Entry permanent residence
posts.metaTitle: Canada Express Entry: Step-by-Step Guide 2026 | iMigrate
posts.metaDescription: Everything you need to know about Canada Express Entry — the three programs, CRS score, the step-by-step process, and how to get an invitation for Canada PR.
posts.content:
Express Entry is the fastest and most popular route to Canadian permanent residence for skilled workers — and with the right strategy, many applicants receive PR within about six months of being invited.

## What is Express Entry?

Express Entry is an online system that manages applications under three federal programs: the **Federal Skilled Worker Program**, the **Canadian Experience Class**, and the **Federal Skilled Trades Program**. Candidates are ranked by a Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) score, and the highest-ranked profiles are invited to apply.

## How the CRS score works

Your CRS score (out of 1,200) is built from core human capital — age, education, language and work experience — plus skill transferability and additional points. A [Provincial Nominee Program](/canada-immigration#pnp) nomination adds **600 points** and effectively guarantees an invitation. Estimate your score with our [CRS calculator](/assessment#calculator).

## The step-by-step process

1. **Sit a language test** (IELTS/CELPIP for English or TEF/TCF for French) and get an **Educational Credential Assessment** for foreign qualifications.
2. **Create your Express Entry profile** and enter the pool with your CRS score.
3. **Receive an Invitation to Apply (ITA)** when your score meets the draw cut-off.
4. **Submit your PR application** within 60 days with full documentation.
5. **Receive your Confirmation of Permanent Residence.**

## How to improve your CRS score

- Improve your language results — even one band higher can add many points
- Add French as a second language for bonus points
- Gain a provincial nomination for a decisive +600
- Secure Canadian study or work experience

## Frequently asked questions

## 1. Express Entry

The fastest federal route for skilled workers, managed through the CRS. Best for candidates with strong language, education and experience. See our [complete Express Entry guide](/blog/canada-express-entry-complete-guide).

## 2. Provincial Nominee Programs (PNP)

Provinces nominate skilled workers who meet local labour-market needs. An enhanced (Express Entry-aligned) nomination adds **600 CRS points**. Ideal if your CRS is below recent cut-offs — explore [Canada PNP options](/canada-immigration#pnp).

## 3. Family sponsorship

Canadian citizens and permanent residents can sponsor a spouse, partner, dependent children, and in some cases parents and grandparents. Learn more about [family sponsorship](/canada-immigration#family-sponsorship).

## 4. Business & start-up immigration

The Start-up Visa Program grants PR to founders of innovative businesses backed by a designated organisation, while the C11 work permit suits entrepreneurs. See [Canada business immigration](/canada-immigration#business-startup).

## Which pathway is right for you?

The best route depends on your CRS score, occupation, ties to a province, and whether you have family in Canada or business experience. Most clients qualify for more than one — we compare them all.

## Frequently asked questions

**What is the easiest way to get Canada PR?**
There's no single "easiest" route — for many skilled workers it's Express Entry, but a PNP can be faster if your CRS is lower.

**How long does Canada PR take?**
Express Entry is often around six months after an ITA; PNP base streams and sponsorship can take a year or more.

Not sure where you fit? [Get a free Canada PR assessment](/assessment) and we'll find your fastest route.

Canada offers more routes to permanent residence than almost any other country, which is great news — but it also means choosing the right pathway matters. Matching your occupation, language ability and goals to the best program can dramatically shorten your journey and improve your odds of success.

## More common questions

### What is the easiest way to get Canada PR?

There's no single easiest route — Express Entry suits high-scoring skilled workers, while a Provincial Nominee Program can help those with in-demand occupations or lower scores.

### Can I get PR without a job offer?

Yes — Express Entry and many PNP streams don't require a job offer, though one can boost your score.

### Does family sponsorship lead to PR?

Yes — Canadian citizens and permanent residents can sponsor a spouse, partner, children and (via the PGP) parents for permanent residence.

### How long does a Canadian immigration application take?

posts.metaDescription: Understand Canada work permits and the LMIA — employer-specific vs open work permits, LMIA-exempt options, the C11 entrepreneur permit, and how work leads to PR.
posts.content:
A Canadian work permit lets you live and work in Canada and build the experience that can lead to permanent residence. Here's how the system works.

## Types of work permits

- **Employer-specific permit** — tied to one employer, job and location (often requires an LMIA)
- **Open work permit** — work for almost any employer (for example, spouses or post-graduation work permit holders)
- **International Mobility Program (IMP)** — LMIA-exempt categories such as intra-company transfers and the [C11 entrepreneur permit](/canada-immigration#business-startup)

## What is an LMIA?

A Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA) is a document a Canadian employer obtains to show that hiring a foreign worker won't negatively affect the local labour market. A positive LMIA can also add CRS points toward [Express Entry](/blog/canada-express-entry-complete-guide).

## How work permits lead to PR

Canadian work experience is highly valuable: it counts toward the Canadian Experience Class and many [Provincial Nominee Programs](/canada-immigration#pnp), and can significantly raise your CRS score.

## Frequently asked questions

**Do I always need an LMIA?**
No — many roles fall under LMIA-exempt IMP categories, such as intra-company transfers or international agreements.

**Can my spouse work in Canada too?**
In many cases your spouse can apply for an open work permit while you hold a valid skilled work permit.

**What is the C11 work permit?**
It's an LMIA-exempt option for entrepreneurs who will own and actively operate a business of significant benefit to Canada.

Want to work in Canada? [Get a free assessment](/assessment) and we'll find the right permit for you.

A Canadian work permit is often the bridge to permanent residence, letting you build the Canadian experience that boosts your Express Entry or PNP profile. Understanding LMIA-based and LMIA-exempt routes helps you and your employer choose the fastest compliant path.

## More common questions

### What is an LMIA?

A Labour Market Impact Assessment is a document an employer obtains to show no Canadian was available for the role, supporting many employer-specific work permits.

### Can a work permit lead to PR?

Yes — skilled Canadian work experience earns CRS points and can qualify you for Express Entry or a Provincial Nominee Program.

### Are there work permits without an LMIA?

Yes — several are LMIA-exempt under international agreements and the International Mobility Program, including the C11 entrepreneur route.

### How long does a Canadian immigration application take?

Timelines vary by program — Express Entry permanent residence is often granted within about six months of an invitation, while work, study and family applications differ. After a free assessment we give you a realistic, personalized timeline.

### Can I include my family in my Canada application?

In most permanent-residence and many temporary pathways your spouse or partner and dependent children can be included, often with study or work rights. We confirm exactly who you can include during your consultation.

- Proof of sufficient funds for tuition and living costs

Study permit holders can usually work part-time during the academic session and full-time during scheduled breaks, subject to current limits — great for gaining Canadian experience and offsetting costs.

After graduating from an eligible program, you may qualify for a **PGWP** of up to three years. This Canadian work experience is highly valuable for the Canadian Experience Class and many [Provincial Nominee Programs](/canada-immigration#pnp).

posts.metaDescription: A practical cost of living guide for newcomers to Canada — rent, transport, groceries, utilities and realistic monthly budgets for singles and families.
posts.content:
Understanding the cost of living helps you plan a confident move to Canada. Here's a realistic breakdown for newcomers.

> Figures are indicative in CAD and vary widely between cities like Toronto, Vancouver, Calgary and smaller centres.

## Housing

Rent is the biggest expense and varies by city. A one-bedroom apartment ranges from roughly $1,400 in smaller cities to $2,500+ in Toronto or Vancouver. Sharing accommodation reduces costs significantly.

## Transport

Monthly public transit passes typically cost $100–$160. Many newcomers rely on transit before buying a car, which adds insurance, fuel and maintenance.

## Groceries and dining

Monthly groceries run about $300–$500 for one person. A casual restaurant meal averages $20–$25.

## Utilities, internet and phone

Utilities are roughly $150–$250 per month (higher in winter), with internet and mobile around $100–$150 combined.

## Monthly budget summary

- **Single person:** approximately $2,000–$3,200 per month
- **Family of four:** approximately $4,500–$6,500 per month

Remember that [Express Entry](/blog/canada-express-entry-complete-guide) requires proof of settlement funds based on family size, so budgeting a buffer is wise.

## Frequently asked questions

**Which Canadian city is most affordable?**
Smaller cities and the Prairies (e.g. parts of Alberta and Manitoba) are generally more affordable than Toronto or Vancouver.

**How much money do I need to move to Canada?**
Beyond visa settlement-fund requirements, three to six months of living costs gives a comfortable cushion.

Planning your move? [Get a free Canada immigration assessment](/assessment) or explore [Canada PR pathways](/canada-immigration).

Planning your budget before you land is one of the smartest things a newcomer can do. Canada's cost of living varies widely between cities — Toronto and Vancouver are pricier, while many Prairie and Atlantic cities offer lower rents and a gentler start for new immigrants.

## More common questions

### How much money do I need to settle in Canada?

Beyond your visa's proof-of-funds requirement, many newcomers budget several months of living costs. Rent is the largest expense and varies sharply by city.

### Which Canadian cities are most affordable?

Cities in the Prairies and Atlantic Canada generally have lower housing costs than Toronto or Vancouver, and several have welcoming regional immigration programs.

### Is healthcare free in Canada?

Public healthcare is publicly funded, though coverage and waiting periods vary by province for new arrivals.

Canadian citizens and permanent residents can sponsor close family members for permanent residence so they can live, work and study in Canada. Here is how each program works and how to give your application the best chance of approval.

## Who you can sponsor

You can sponsor a **spouse or common-law partner**, **dependent children**, and — through the Parents and Grandparents Program (PGP) — your **parents and grandparents**. In limited cases you may also sponsor an orphaned sibling, niece or nephew.

## Spouse and partner sponsorship

This is the most common route. As a sponsor you must show your relationship is genuine and commit to financially supporting your partner. Applications can be submitted from inside or outside Canada, and many spouses qualify for an open work permit while the application is processed.

## Parents and Grandparents Program

The PGP runs through an invitation system, and the **Super Visa** is a strong alternative that lets parents and grandparents visit for up to five years at a time. We help families compare both options during a [free eligibility assessment](/assessment).

## Income and undertaking requirements

Sponsors must usually meet a minimum necessary income (especially for the PGP) and sign an undertaking to support the sponsored relative for a set period. Getting these details right is where many applications fail.

## Start your sponsorship the right way

Family applications are document-heavy and unforgiving of errors. Explore our [Canada immigration services](/canada-immigration) or [contact our team](/contact) to plan your sponsorship with confidence.

Family sponsorship is one of the most rewarding immigration routes — reuniting partners, children and parents as permanent residents. Because these applications are document-heavy and assessed closely for genuineness, careful preparation is the difference between a smooth approval and a stressful delay.

## Frequently asked questions

### Who can I sponsor for Canada PR?

You can sponsor a spouse or common-law partner, dependent children, and (through the Parents and Grandparents Program) your parents and grandparents.

### How long does spousal sponsorship take?

Processing varies, but many spouse applications take roughly 12 months. An open work permit may be available while you wait.

### What is the Super Visa?

The Super Visa lets parents and grandparents visit for up to five years at a time and is a popular alternative when PGP invitations are limited.

### How long does a Canadian immigration application take?

Timelines vary by program — Express Entry permanent residence is often granted within about six months of an invitation, while work, study and family applications differ. After a free assessment we give you a realistic, personalized timeline.

posts.metaDescription: Becoming a Canadian citizen after PR — physical presence, language and tax requirements, the citizenship test and the step-by-step application process.
posts.content:
Canadian citizenship gives you a Canadian passport, the right to vote, and the security of a status that cannot be lost through time spent abroad. Most people apply after several years as a permanent resident. Here is what you need to qualify.

## Physical presence requirement

You must have been physically present in Canada for at least **1,095 days (three years) within the five years** before you apply. Some time spent in Canada before becoming a PR can count partially. Keeping an accurate travel history is essential.

## Language and knowledge

Applicants aged 18–54 must show **English or French ability** (usually CLB 4 or higher) and pass a **citizenship test** covering Canada's history, geography, rights and responsibilities.

## Tax filing and good standing

You generally need to have filed income taxes for the required years and have no unresolved immigration or criminal issues.

## The application process

You submit your application with proof of presence, language and identity, complete the test and (if required) an interview, then take the Oath of Citizenship. Processing times vary, so apply as soon as you are eligible.

## Not a PR yet?

Citizenship starts with permanent residence. If you are still planning your move, review your options through [Canada immigration](/canada-immigration) or book a [free assessment](/assessment) with our consultants.

Canadian citizenship is the final milestone of the immigration journey, giving you a passport, the right to vote and a status that can't be lost through time abroad. Meeting the physical-presence, language and tax requirements is straightforward with good record-keeping and forward planning.

## Frequently asked questions

### How long must I live in Canada before applying for citizenship?

You must be physically present for at least 1,095 days (three years) within the five years before you apply.

### Is there a citizenship test?

Yes — applicants aged 18–54 take a test on Canada's history, geography, rights and responsibilities, and must show English or French ability.

### Can I hold dual citizenship in Canada?

Canada permits dual citizenship, though you should confirm whether your home country does too.

### How long does a Canadian immigration application take?

Timelines vary by program — Express Entry permanent residence is often granted within about six months of an invitation, while work, study and family applications differ. After a free assessment we give you a realistic, personalized timeline.

### Can I include my family in my Canada application?

In most permanent-residence and many temporary pathways your spouse or partner and dependent children can be included, often with study or work rights. We confirm exactly who you can include during your consultation.

posts.content:
The Partner Visa lets the spouse or de facto partner of an Australian citizen, permanent resident or eligible New Zealand citizen live in Australia permanently. Onshore applicants apply for the **Subclass 820/801**; offshore applicants use the **309/100**.

## A two-stage visa

You apply for the temporary (820) and permanent (801) stages together. The temporary visa lets you live and work in Australia while your relationship is assessed; the permanent visa is usually granted around two years later if the relationship continues.

## Proving a genuine relationship

This is the heart of every Partner Visa. The Department looks at four pillars: **financial aspects, the nature of the household, social recognition, and commitment to each other**. Strong, consistent evidence across all four is what gets applications approved.

## De facto relationships

If you are not married, you generally need to show you have lived together (or been in a genuine relationship) for at least 12 months, unless you register the relationship in an eligible state.

## Common pitfalls

Thin relationship evidence, gaps in your timeline, and weak statements are the most frequent reasons for delay or refusal. Preparation matters.

## Plan your Partner Visa

Every couple's situation is different. See our [Australia migration services](/australia-migration) or [get a free assessment](/assessment) to map out the strongest possible application.

The Partner Visa lets the spouse or de facto partner of an Australian citizen, permanent resident or eligible New Zealand citizen settle permanently in Australia. Success hinges on consistent, well-organised evidence of a genuine and continuing relationship across four key areas.

## Frequently asked questions

### What are the four pillars of a genuine relationship?

The Department assesses your financial aspects, the nature of your household, social recognition of the relationship, and your mutual commitment.

### Can I work on a Partner Visa?

Yes — the temporary (820) stage grants full work rights while your relationship is assessed.

### How long until I get permanent residence?

The permanent (801) stage is usually decided around two years after lodgement if the relationship continues.

### How long does an Australian visa application take?

Processing depends on the visa and your profile — points-tested skilled visas can take several months after an invitation. After a free assessment we provide a realistic, personalized timeline rather than a generic estimate.

### Can my family be included in my Australian visa?

Most skilled, partner and employer-sponsored visas let you include your partner and dependent children, who can usually study and work. We confirm your family's eligibility during your assessment.

posts.content:
A Canadian work permit lets you gain valuable Canadian experience that often becomes a springboard to permanent residence. There are two broad categories — and choosing the right one is key.

## Open work permits

An **open work permit** lets you work for almost any employer in Canada. Common holders include spouses of skilled workers and international students, and post-graduation work permit (PGWP) holders. There is no job offer or LMIA required.

## Employer-specific work permits

An **employer-specific (closed) permit** ties you to one employer and role. Many of these require a positive **Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA)**, where the employer shows no Canadian was available for the job. Others are LMIA-exempt under international agreements.

## The C11 entrepreneur route

Business owners may qualify for a work permit by starting or buying a Canadian business. Learn more in our dedicated [C11 Entrepreneur Work Permit](/c11-entrepreneur-work-permit) guide.

## From work permit to PR

Skilled Canadian work experience adds CRS points and can qualify you for Express Entry or a Provincial Nominee Program. A work permit is rarely the destination — it is the bridge.

## Find your best route

Explore [Canada immigration options](/canada-immigration) or take a [free eligibility assessment](/assessment) to see which work permit fits your profile.

### What is an open work permit?

An open work permit lets you work for almost any employer in Canada — common for spouses of skilled workers and post-graduation work permit holders.

### What is an employer-specific work permit?

It ties you to one employer and role, and often requires a positive LMIA or an LMIA exemption.

### Can a work permit lead to PR?

Yes — Canadian work experience adds CRS points and can qualify you for Express Entry or a Provincial Nominee Program.

posts.metaTitle: How to Keep Your PR: Residency Obligations | iMigrate
posts.metaDescription: Permanent residence comes with stay requirements. Learn the Canada and Australia residency obligations and how to avoid losing your hard-won PR status.
posts.content:
Earning permanent residence is a milestone — but PR is not unconditional. Both Canada and Australia expect you to actually live in the country. Here is how to protect your status.

## Canada's residency obligation

To keep PR and renew your **PR card**, you must be physically present in Canada for at least **730 days (two years) in every rolling five-year period**. Time accompanying a Canadian-citizen spouse abroad can count in some cases.

## Australia's travel facility

Australian PR includes a **five-year travel facility**. To re-enter after it expires, you generally need a **Resident Return Visa (RRV)**, which considers your ties and time spent in Australia.

## Plan long absences carefully

If work or family requires extended time abroad, plan ahead. Keep evidence of your ties — property, employment, family — in case you need to demonstrate them.

## The path to citizenship

The surest way to remove residency worries is to become a citizen once eligible, since citizenship is not affected by time abroad.

## Need advice on your status?

If you are worried about meeting your obligation or renewing your card, [contact our team](/contact) or explore our [immigration services](/services) for tailored guidance.

Permanent residence is a privilege with conditions — both Canada and Australia expect you to actually live there. Understanding the residency obligations, and planning long absences carefully, protects the status you worked so hard to earn.

You must be physically present in Canada at least 730 days (two years) in every rolling five-year period to keep PR and renew your PR card.

Australian PR includes a five-year travel facility; to re-enter after it expires you generally need a Resident Return Visa.

posts.content:
Your **Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS)** score decides whether you receive an invitation to apply for Canadian PR. If you are below recent cut-offs, several proven levers can lift your score.

## Boost your language scores

Language is the highest-value, most controllable factor. Retaking IELTS or CELPIP to reach **CLB 9+** can add dozens of points, including skill-transferability bonuses.

## Add education or credentials

Getting an **Educational Credential Assessment (ECA)** for foreign degrees, or completing further study, can increase your points.

## Provincial Nomination = 600 points
